coredata.dc:title: Full-wood photoluminescent and photothermic materials for thermal energy storage
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Haiyue
Liu, Yushan
Li, Jian
Wang, Chengyu
Li, Yudong
coredata.dc:description: Due to its unique structure and component, wood is widely used as matrix to develop functional composites. However, the lignin is removed from wood and thrown away as waste in most wood-based functional composites. In this work, a new strategy of “taken from the wood, back into the wood” to fabricate a full-wood photoluminescent and photothermic material is proposed. By facile delignification and solvothermal process, the delignified wood (DW) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from removal lignin are prepared. In addition, compared with other CQDs excited by UV light, the CQDs possess red (650 nm) and NIR (710 nm) emission light under visible light excitation (580 nm). Most importantly, the heat radiated from NIR emission could be stored by the introduced phase change materials (PCM) to adjust ambient temperature. The composite owns photoluminescent, photothermic and energy storage property (heat latent of 137.6 J/g), which is environmentally friendly, green and creative. The all-new technique for functional wood composite shows potential for application in energy-saving and optical devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Full-wood
Carbon quantum dots
Phase change materials
Photoluminescent
Photothermic

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic for the preparation of full-wood photoluminescent and photothermic materials. The principle of “taken from the wood, back into the wood” is adopted in the experiment. After delignification, the DW preserves the unique porous structure. And the removed lignin as the precursor, red and NIR emissive fluorescent CQDs are synthesized by a facile solvothermal method. After the PEG@CQDs compound is filled in, the full-wood photoluminescent and photothermic materials are obtained. The NIR emission light could generate heat by radiation, and the heat can be stored by phase change materials for adjust ambient temperature and energy saving.

a) Schematic illustration of the preparation of CQDs derived from lignin. b) TEM image (Inset: HRTEM image and size distribution) of CQDs. c) UV–vis absorption (the inset photographs are under sunlight and under UV light), d) FL spectra under different excitation wavelengths, e) FL emission spectra at different concentrations under 580 nm excitation of CQDs.

SEM images of cross-sectional a) DW, b) PEG/DW, and c) PEG@CQDs-5/DW composite. The insets in (a-c) show the optical images of DW, PEG/DW, and PEG@CQDs-5/DW composite, respectively. d) FL spectra of PEG@CQDs-5/DW under different excitation wavelengths. e) FL spectra of PEG@CQDs/DW with different CQDs concentration under 580 nm excitation. f) FL spectra of PEG@CQDs-5/DW at different temperature.

a) DSC curves of pure PEG, PEG/DW, PEG@CQDs-5/DW composite. b) DSC curves of PEG@CQDs-5/DW before and after 100 heating-cooling cycles. c) TGA curves and d) corresponding DTG curves of DW, PEG, CQDs, PEG/DW, PEG@CQDs-5/DW. e) IR images of PEG@CQDs-5/DW under 580 nm laser irradiation recorded with an IR camera, and photothermal effect: temperature-time curves of PEG/DW, CQDs-5/DW and PEG@CQDs-5/DW composite.
Leakage test of pure PEG, PEG/DW, PEG@CQDs-5/DW at 20 °C and 60 °C.
Thermal properties of PEG 2000, PEG/DW, and PEG@CQDs-5/DW composites.

head.ce:title: Full-wood photoluminescent and photothermic materials for thermal energy storage

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science and Technology, Ministry of Education, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science and Technology, Ministry of Education, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this study and share first authorship.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to its unique structure and component, wood is widely used as matrix to develop functional composites. However, the lignin is removed from wood and thrown away as waste in most wood-based functional composites. In this work, a new strategy of “taken from the wood, back into the wood” to fabricate a full-wood photoluminescent and photothermic material is proposed. By facile delignification and solvothermal process, the delignified wood (DW) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from removal lignin are prepared. In addition, compared with other CQDs excited by UV light, the CQDs possess red (650 nm) and NIR (710 nm) emission light under visible light excitation (580 nm). Most importantly, the heat radiated from NIR emission could be stored by the introduced phase change materials (PCM) to adjust ambient temperature. The composite owns photoluminescent, photothermic and energy storage property (heat latent of 137.6 J/g), which is environmentally friendly, green and creative. The all-new technique for functional wood composite shows potential for application in energy-saving and optical devices.

ce:keyword.ce:text: the full-wood photoluminescent and photothermic composites with different content of CQDs
the composite phase change materials without CQDs
the full-wood photoluminescent and photothermic composites after the 100 heating-cooling cycles

ce:section.ce:para: Phase change materials (PCMs), as promising thermal energy storage devices, are drawing much attention due to their high energy storage capacity, small volume change, good thermal stability, long cycle life, and nontoxicity [1–4]. PCMs have been widely applied in many field, such as clothes [5], cylindrical power battery pack [6,7], the wall of building [8–10], heating radiator [11], solar air heater [12], storage box for food and medicine [13], which can adjust the ambient temperature and reduce energy consumption in practical application, due to heat energy storage and release during phase change process. However, one critical issue hindering their practical application is liquid leakage problem during phase change process [14,15]. Porous materials have been considered as most promising candidate used for supporting materials to address the liquid leakage issue, such as hydroxyapatite [16], diatomite [17], melamine foam [18], aerogel [19], carbon foam [2,20], and MOFs [21] et al.. On the basis of solving the liquid leakage issue, researchers are pursuing two hotspots in the field of phase change materials. (1) Specifically, ideal supporting materials with low density, high porosity, excellent mechanical property to improve thermal energy storage density. (2) Advanced multifunctional composite phase change materials to meet higher requirements in wide applications.

Wood can be used as supporting materials due to its unique porous structure, high mechanical strength, abundant sources, low cost, environmentally friendly and biodegradable [22–25]. In addition, wood has been widely applied in decoration, building, bridge, furniture, and tool fields. Therefore, wood-based composite phase change materials broaden the application range of PCMs and provide more possibility for practical application in the future. Three familiar styles of wood (poplar wood [26], basswood [27], and balsa wood [28]) were used as supporting materials for composite phase change materials. Among them, the loading ratio (88.34%) of balsa wood is largest, 28.3% and 23.34% higher than basswood and poplar wood, respectively. This is attributed to lowest density of balsa wood. In addition, wang and co-workers fabricated delignified wood-based composite phase change materials with reversible thermochromic ability, which demonstrated that delignification process is helpful to improve the loading ratio of PCMs [26]. Therefore, delignified balsa wood could be used as ideal supporting materials to improve thermal energy storage capacity.

In delignification process, the removed lignin is usually treated as waste residue with low utilization. However, lignin possesses rich aromatic structure and high carbon content, which is favorable as precursor for carbon-based nanomaterials [29–32]. Recently, fluorescence carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from lignin have been developed. Li et al. synthesized a blue-fluorescent N-doped CQDs via hydrothermal carbonization of lignin with ammonia water [33]. Rai et al. reported blue emissive CQDs from lignin under microwave irradiation [34]. Chen et al. prepared blue fluorescent CQDs by hydrothermal treatment of lignin in the presence of H2O2 [35]. Although they exhibit excellent luminescence properties, most only excite under UV-light and emit blue or green light. Nevertheless, UV-light is harmful and less abundant than visible light in sunlight. Thus, CQDs excited by visible light are urgently needed in practical application.

For the first time, a full-wood composite concept, based on the principle of “taken from the wood, back into the wood” is proposed and designed. After a facile delignification process, the removed lignin as precursor is prepared into CQDs by solvothermal method. Then, the fluorescent CQDs and phase change materials are impregnated into delignified wood to fabricate a multifunctional full-wood photoluminescent and photothermic material for thermal energy storage (Fig. 1 ). The CQDs exhibit strong red and NIR luminescence with emission at 650 nm with a shoulder peak at 710 nm under 580 nm (visible light) excitation. Interestingly, the composites possess photothermal property under a 580 nm laser, because NIR light emission of CQDs can radiate heat. Introducing phase change materials to the full-wood composite, the heat generated by NIR radiation could be absorbed and stored by phase change materials (PCM) to adjust the surrounding environment and thereby save energy. In addition, delignified wood (DW) as supporting materials could address the liquid leakage issue of PCM during phase change process due to the capillary effect and surface tension. The novel multifunctional full-wood photoluminescent and photothermic materials have important potential application in decoration, building, furniture and storage box for advanced energy storage and optical devices.

Balsa wood (with dimensions of 30 mm × 30 mm × 3 mm) was used in the study. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, >96%), sodium sulphite (Na2SO3, >98%), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%), polyethylene glycol (PEG, with a molecular weight of 2000 g/mol), o-phenylenediamine (OPDA) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 40%), phosphoric acid (H3PO4), hydrochloric acid (HCl) and anhydrous ethanol were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. And all chemical reagents were used as received without any purification.

The balsa wood was immersed into aqueous solution (2.5 M NaOH and 0.4 M Na2SO3) and boiled for 12 h, followed by rinsing in hot water for five times to remove most of chemicals. And the brown lignin removal solution cools for further use. Then, the wood samples were bleached in boiling 2.5 M H2O2 solution for 4 h until they turned white, further remove the remaining small amount of lignin. The white wood samples were washed five times with deionized water, and freeze-dried for 1 day.

The pH of brown lignin removal solution was adjusted to 2 using 2 M HCl solution, to precipitate lignin in acidic environment. And the lignin was separated by vacuum suction filtration and washed with deionized water until neutral pH. Finally, the purified lignin was dried in an oven at 50 °C for 48 h.

The purified lignin (0.1 g), o-phenylenediamine (0.3 g) were dissolved in solution of 40% H2SO4 (5 mL) and H3PO4 (5 mL) with stirring. And the mixture was transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene)(Teflon)-lined autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 220 °C for 10 h. After that, the reactor was cooled to room temperature naturally. The reaction solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min to remove solid impurity. Next, the supernatant is dialyzed in deionized water (retained molecular weight: 1000 Da) for 48 h. The CQDs were obtained after drying in an oven at 70 °C.

1 mL, 2 mL, 5 mL and 8 mL CQDs solution (1.2 mg/mL in anhydrous ethanol) was added into 30 g melted PEG 2000 and kept stirring at 70 °C for 20 min. Then the delignified wood was infiltrated in PEG@CQDs solution under vacuum at 70 °C for 30 min. To ensure the full infiltration, the vacuum-assisted impregnation process was repeated for three times. The composite phase change materials were thus obtained. And the corresponding composites were named as PEG@CQDs-1/DW, PEG@CQDs-2/DW, PEG@CQDs-5/DW, and PEG@CQDs-8/DW composites respectively. In addition, PEG/DW composite and CQDs-5/DW composite (5 mL CQDs solution added into 30 mL ethanol) were fabricated by the same method as that of PEG@CQDs/DW composite. In order to clearly show the properties of the PEG@CQDs/DW composites, PEG@CQDs-5/DW is used as an example for the following characterization.

Before characterization of all samples, the liquid leakage test was performed as follows: all samples were put on weighing papers in an oven at 60 °C for 30 min (above the melting point of PEG 2000), respectively. Then all weighing papers were weighed and compared before and after the test, to judge if the liquid leakage happened.

The morphologies of CQDs were observed by a JEM2100 TEM at 200 kV. FTIR spectra were recorded on Thermo Fisher Scientific Nicolet 6700 over the range of 400–4000 cm−1. XRD pattern was collected by a D8 X-ray diffractometer. XPS spectra were investigated using an Escalab 250 XI spectrometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained by a TU-1950 UV–vis spectrometer. Photoluminescence measurements were conducted by an Agilent Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with a 150 W xenon lamp as the excitation source. Fluorescent lifetime measurements were recorded using a FluoroLog-3 fluorescence spectrophotometer, with excitation/emission wavelengths of 580/650 nm. The thermal properties were measured by differential scanning calorimeter (DSC, Q20) and thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, Q20). The photothermic test was performed by a 580 nm laser (MRL-III-FS-580 nm-1.0 W), and the temperature is recorded by infrared thermal camera (FLIR, E6).

The CQDs were prepared using the removed lignin and OPDA as precursor by simple solvothermal method (Fig. 2 a). The morphologies of CQDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 2b, the CQDs are well dispersed with the average diameter of 8.66 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image confirms the crystalline structure of CQDs with interlayer spacing of 0.24 nm, which corresponds to the (100) lattice planes of graphene (the inset of Fig. 2b) [36,37]. The X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) exhibits a peak at 18.05° attributed to the (002) crystal plane of graphitic structure (Fig. S1) [38]. The compositions of CQDs are investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Fig. S2) and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR, Fig. S3). XPS spectra (Fig. S2) show three typical peak of C 1s (285 eV), N 1s (400 eV), and O 1s (531 eV), which investigate the presence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen in CQDs [39,40]. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S3) shows the presence of N/OH, CH, CO, CC, COC, CH, CN, NH, which is consistent with XPS results.

The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CQDs (derived from the removed lignin) exhibits broad absorption bands at 234–360 nm and 420–650 nm, which indicates CQDs possess significant absorption in both the UV and visible light regions. The reason could be corresponded to the
π →
π
∗
transistion of the aromatis CC bond, and n →
π
∗
transitions of the aromatic sp2 system containing CO and CN bonds, respectively. The CQDs show strong red fluorescence emission under UV light excitation (Fig. 2c and inset). There are all two emission peaks at 650 nm (red light) and 710 nm (NIR light) under different excitation wavelength, and these emission peaks do not shift, which suggests CQDs exhibit excitation-independent fluorescence (FL) emission. The optimum excitation wavelength appears at 580 nm (Fig. 2d). The FL emission behavior of CQDs at different concentrations shows in the Fig. 2e, and the FL emission intensity increases as concentration increasing. A typical fluorescence decay curve of CQDs presents the FL lifetime is 2.1 ns (Fig. S4). All above results demonstrate that CQDs possess excellent and uniform FL properties, and the unique red and NIR emission is potential in photothermic field.

The microstructure of DW, PEG/DW, PEG@CQDs-5/DW are presented in Fig. 3 a-c. The DW owns an anisotropic porous structure, well-preserved its inherent structure before delignification process. The inset of Fig. 3a shows the picture of white delignified wood block after lignin removal. After PEG infiltration, the wood vessels are completely filled with PEG, and no obvious interface is observed between the cell walls and PEG due to the hydrogen-bond interaction (Fig. 3b), which is beneficial for preventing leakage of PEG. When PEG@CQDs compound was filled in the wood, the microstructure is no obviously different from PEG/DW composite, which derived from CQDs nanoparticles with 8.66 nm average diameter. But the color of PEG@CQDs-5/DW is red in the inset of Fig. 3c, which displays CQDs are evenly distributed in the composite. All characteristic peaks of PEG, CQDs, and DW appear in the XRD and FT-IR spectra of PEG@CQDs-5/DW (Fig. S5 and S6), which demonstrate that PEG@CQDs compound is successfully incorporated into DW.

Compared with PEG/DW composite, PEG@CQDs-5/DW possesses an absorption peak at 440–730 nm, due to the addition of CQDs. And the inset shows the actual picture of PEG@CQDs-5/DW under sunlight and UV light, and the composite can emit red luminescence under 365 nm excitation (Fig. S7). The FL properties of PEG@CQDs-5/DW composite are consistent with that of pure CQDs. There are two emission peaks at 650 and 710 nm with excitation-independent fluorescence emission (Fig. 3d). The FL intensity increase as the concentration of CQDs increasing, and the PEG/DW composite is unable to exhibit fluorescence emission under 580 nm excitation (Fig. 3e). Interestingly, the FL intensity of PEG@CQDs-5/DW changes at different temperature. The FL intensity at 25 °C is higher than that at 60 °C, which is ascribed to phase change process of PEG from solid state to liquid state. When PEG is solid, CQDs would aggregate together to improve relative concentration, which is consistent with the result of Fig. 3e. Thus, the PEG@CQDs-5/DW composite possesses temperature-responsive property, in which the phase change process could be determined by FL intensity. In addition, the FL lifetime of PEG@CQDs-5/DW composite is 2.74 ns (Fig. S8), which is delayed by 0.64 ns than pure CQDs, because of the hydrogen bonding between PEG and CQDs.

Fig. 4 a shows the phase change behaviors of pure PEG and composite PCMs and the corresponding thermal properties are summarized in Table 1 . The melting and freezing temperatures of pure PEG2000 are 50.48 °C and 36.98 °C, respectively. The PEG/DW and PEG@CQDs/DW composites own similar melting and freezing temperature, which indicates the incorporation of DW and CQDs affects little on the phase change temperature of PEG2000. The pure PEG2000 possesses a high fusion enthalpy (165.5 J/g), disclosing PEG2000 is an excellent candidate for thermal energy storage. PEG/DW composite exhibits a lower fusion enthalpy than that of pure PEG2000, since DW works as supporting materials, not as working substance.

After adding the CQDs, the fusion enthalpy of PEG@CQDs/DW decreases with the increase of CQDs (Table 1 and Fig. S9). By giving a comprehensive account of FL intensity and fusion enthalpy, PEG@CQDs-5/DW was chosen to perform all the following investigations. The fusion enthalpy of PEG@CQDs-5/DW is 0.9 J/g less than that of PEG/DW, whereas PEG@CQDs-5/DW still exhibits a high fusion enthalpy up to 150.0 J/g. The content of PEG in PEG@CQDs-5/DW is 90.63 wt% calculated from Table 1, which suggests that PEG@CQDs-5/DW possesses outstanding thermal energy storage capacity. To measure the reversibility of PEG@CQDs-5/DW, the 100 heating–cooling cycling tests were carried out, and the results are shown in Fig. 4b and Table 1. Compared with the phase change temperature and enthalpy of fusion before cycles, the PEG@CQDs-5/DW100 exhibits similar phase change temperature and the enthalpy of fusion is only reduced by 6.1 J/g, which is reasonable during heating–cooling cycles. This demonstrates that PEG@CQDs-5/DW owns long cycle times and service life.

Fig. 4c and d show the TGA and DTG curves of DW, PEG, CQDs, PEG/DW, PEG@CQDs-5/DW. DW exhibits one-step thermal degradation, beginning at about 200 °C and ending at 330 °C, with maximum weight loss rate at 305 °C. Only one-step occurring at temperature range between 310 °C and 400 °C belongs to the thermal degradation of PEG. There are two-step degradations on the DTG curve of CQDs at about 60 °C and 375 °C. The degradation occurring at 60 °C owes to adsorbed water, and the real degradation temperature of CQDs is about 375 °C. The preparation temperature of CQDs is 220 °C, thus, the thermal degradation temperature cannot be lower than 220 °C. For PEG@CQDs-5/DW, there are two obvious degradation processes, which attribute to the degradation of DW and PEG. Therefore, PEG@CQDs-5/DW exhibits thermal stability below 200 °C, which implies PEG@CQDs-5/DW composite should be used in practical application below 200 °C.

To investigate photothermal ability of PEG@CQDs-5/DW, the composites are placed under 580 nm laser (1 W cm−2), and the temperature is recorded by infrared thermal camera. Under the laser radiation, the temperature of PEG/DW only increases by 1.9 °C from 25.9 °C to 27.8 °C for 40 min irradiation (Fig. S10). However, the temperature of PEG@CQDs-5/DW rises to 78.5 °C from room temperature for 390 s (Fig. 4e). The reason is that PEG@CQDs-5/DW exhibits emission peaks at 650 nm (red) and 710 nm (NIR) under 580 nm excitation and the NIR fluorescence emission could radiate heat energy, because of the addition of CQDs. There is no emission peak on PEG/DW under 580 nm laser. This shows that the increase of temperature is not caused by the laser, but by the NIR radiation [41]. Interestingly, there is a plateau appearing on the curve of PEG@CQDs-5/DW between 49 °C and 50 °C, indicating that the solid–liquid phase change process of PEG takes place and the radiant heat energy is stored. When the laser turns off, the temperature drops rapidly. In the same way, a freezing plateau between 38 °C and 35 °C appears and it lasts for 930 s. This is corresponding to the solidification temperature of PEG@CQDs-5/DW (Table 1), and plenty of stored heat is released to adjust the surrounding temperature. It takes only 75 s from 25.6 °C to 80.9 °C for CQDs-5/DW, which is less than heating time of PEG@CQDs-5/DW. The reason is that there is no energy storage substance and no plateau in heating and cooling stage for CQDs-5/DW (Fig. 4e). In addition, Fig. S11 shows the photothermal effect of PEG@CQDs/DW composite with different content of CQDs. They have similar temperature rise behavior, but the heating rate of PEG@CQDs/DW increases with content of CQDs. These results demonstrate that PEG@CQDs/DW possesses efficient photothermal ability and thermal energy storage capacity.

The liquid leakage problem of PEG hinders its practical application. Therefore, the leakage test is critical for the application of composite. Fig. 5 shows the picture of pure PEG, PEG/DW, and PEG@CQDs-5/DW at 20 and 60 °C. When the temperature is above melting temperature of PEG, pure PEG turns into liquid. However, there is no liquid leakage observed in PEG/DW and PEG@CQDs-5/DW composite. The reason is that DW as supporting material prevents liquid leakage due to the capillary effect and surface tension. This demonstrates PEG@CQDs-5/DW possesses excellent shape stability, which is favorable in practical applications.

In summary, a full-wood photoluminescent and photothermic material with delignified wood as supporting material, CQDs derived from the removed lignin as backfill fluorescent material, and PEG as phase change materials is reported. Compare with other CQDs excited by UV light, the CQDs exhibit two emission peaks at 650 and 710 nm (red and NIR light) under 580 nm excitation (visible light). Additionally, the PEG@CQDs/DW composites possess the same fluorescent property with pure CQDs, and the FL intensity decrease with the increase of temperature due to solid–liquid transition of PEG. Specially, the composites exhibit outstanding photothermal conversion efficiency under NIR emissive light irradiation. And the radiant heat could be stored by phase change materials for adjusting ambient temperature and saving energy. Furthermore, the composites possess large energy storage capacity, long cycle life, excellent form stability and thermal stability below 200 °C. The full-wood photoluminescent and photothermic materials are promising for advanced energy storage and optical devices. The principle of “taken from the wood, back into the wood” also open up for exploring new functional composites, which is environmentally friendly, green and creative.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. 31770605, 31822008), the Outstanding Youth Foundation of Heilongjiang (Grant no. JC2018006), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant no. 2572019BB04).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2020.126406.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Coal-based ultrathin-wall graphitic porous carbon for high-performance form-stable phase change materials with enhanced thermal conductivity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Zhu, Q. Wang, S. Kang, J. Li, X. Jia, Coal-based ultrathin-wall graphitic porous carbon for high-performance form-stable phase change materials with enhanced thermal conductivity, Chem Eng J, 395 (2020) 125112.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hierarchically channel-guided porous wood-derived shape-stabilized thermal regulated materials with enhanced thermal conductivity for thermal energy storage

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhao, B. Sun, P. Du, X. Min, Z. Huang, Y.g. Liu, X. Wu, M. Fang, Hierarchically channel-guided porous wood-derived shape-stabilized thermal regulated materials with enhanced thermal conductivity for thermal energy storage, Mater Res Express, 6 (2019) 115515.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Phase change materials for thermal energy storage

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Pielichowska, K. Pielichowski, Phase change materials for thermal energy storage, Prog Mater Sci, 65 (2014) 67-123.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High latent heat and recyclable form-stable phase change materials prepared via a facile self-template method

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Tan, Y. Xiao, R. Chen, C. Zhou, L. Wang, Y. Liu, D. Li, High latent heat and recyclable form-stable phase change materials prepared via a facile self-template method, Chem Eng J, 396 (2020) 125265.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Encapsulation of phase change materials by complex coacervation to improve thermal performances of woven fabrics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Onder, N. Sarier, E. Cimen, Encapsulation of phase change materials by complex coacervation to improve thermal performances of woven fabrics, Thermochim Acta, 467 (2008) 63-72.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Experimental study on the thermal management performance of phase change material coupled with heat pipe for cylindrical power battery pack

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Zhao, P. Lv, Z. Rao, Experimental study on the thermal management performance of phase change material coupled with heat pipe for cylindrical power battery pack, Exp Therm Fluid Sci, 82 (2017) 182-188.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preventing thermal runaway propagation in lithium ion battery packs using a phase change composite material: An experimental study

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Wilke, B. Schweitzer, S. Khateeb, S. Al-Hallaj, Preventing thermal runaway propagation in lithium ion battery packs using a phase change composite material: An experimental study, J Power Sources, 340 (2017) 51-59.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Numerical and experimental investigation of an insulation layer with phase change materials (PCMs)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Fateh, F. Klinker, M. Brütting, H. Weinläder, F. Devia, Numerical and experimental investigation of an insulation layer with phase change materials (PCMs), Energ Buildings, 153 (2017) 231-240.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thermal performance of buildings integrated with phase change materials to reduce heat stress risks during extreme heatwave events

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Ramakrishnan, X. Wang, J. Sanjayan, J. Wilson, Thermal performance of buildings integrated with phase change materials to reduce heat stress risks during extreme heatwave events, Appl Energ, 194 (2017) 410-421.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Phase change materials and products for building applications: a state-of-the-art review and future research opportunities

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.E. Kalnæs, B.P. Jelle, Phase change materials and products for building applications: A state-of-the-art review and future research opportunities, Energ Buildings, 94 (2015) 150-176.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thermal performance of phase change material (PCM) based pin-finned heat sinks for electronics devices: effect of pin thickness and PCM volume fraction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Arshad, H.M. Ali, M. Ali, S. Manzoor, Thermal performance of phase change material (PCM) based pin-finned heat sinks for electronics devices: Effect of pin thickness and PCM volume fraction, Appl Therm Eng, 112 (2017) 143-155.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Optimization of a solar air heater with phase change materials: Experimental and numerical study

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Moradi, A. Kianifar, S. Wongwises, Optimization of a solar air heater with phase change materials: Experimental and numerical study, Exp Therm Fluid Sci, 89 (2017) 41-49.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Review on phase change materials (PCMs) for cold thermal energy storage applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Oró, A. de Gracia, A. Castell, M.M. Farid, L.F. Cabeza, Review on phase change materials (PCMs) for cold thermal energy storage applications, Appl Energ, 99 (2012) 513-533.
